front: Composer: member of the Mighty Five; published Principles of Orchestration (using only his own works as examples); taught Stravinsky; completed Mussorgsky's Night on Bald Mountain
Composer: described the bassoon as 'an atmosphere of senile mockery' in his Principles of Orchestration; Stravinsky borrowed folk melodies from his collection 100 Russian Folk Songs for The Firebird

Tone Poem: Scheherazade represented by solo violin with harp playing high sixteenth-note triplets; the Sultan by an E-D-C-B-flat bass motif; four movements including 'The Sea and Sinbad's Ship' and 'Festival at Baghdad'
Tone Poem: his wife protested its 1910 adaptation as a ballet by Michel Fokine and Léon Bakst

Tone Poem: five movements including two 'Alborada' sections (identical except for key and orchestration), 'Scena e canto gitano' (five cadenzas over snare drum), and 'Fandango asturiano' with castanets
Overture: opens in 5/2 time with a Lento mystico liturgical melody; based on Obikhod chants; ends with 'Resurrexit!' exclamations shifting into 2/1 time
Opera: rapid descending chromatic runs in the flutes depict Prince Gvidon's transformation into an insect by the Swan-Bird
back: The Tale of Tsar Saltan – 'Flight of the Bumblebee' (Rimsky-Korsakov)

front: Opera: contains three 'Guest Songs' for Varangian, Indian ('Song of India'), and Venetian visitors; Tommy Dorsey adapted the 'Song of India' into a jazz trombone standard
Opera: the Snow Maiden is wooed by Lel and Mizgir; declares her love for Mizgir and melts in the spring sunlight; includes the 'Dance of the Tumblers'
Opera: satirical opera immediately banned when published; requires a rare tenor altino voice for the role of the Astrologer (a eunuch)
